Title: Provitamin A biofortification of cassava enhances shelf life but reduces dry matter content of storage roots due to altered carbon partitioning into starch

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: potato granule-bound starch synthase promoter (storage organ-associated)
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Only modestly increased carotenoid accumulation in cassava storage roots (~3–4 µg/g DW), much less effective than crtB or crtB+DXS
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Expression of mutant Or in cassava roots caused only low carotenoid accumulation. 2. Maximum total carotenoids were ~2-fold above control, far below DXS//crtB lines. 3. The strategy was therefore less effective for cassava provitamin A biofortification in this study.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic expression in cassava and carotenoid quantification by spectrophotometry
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited standalone value in cassava based on this study; inferior to crtB+DXS strategy
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as one of the three tested engineering strategies, but not a major successful driver of the reported high provitamin A phenotype.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Down-regulated in carotenoid-accumulating storage roots relative to nontransgenic control at 12 MAP
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced expression is associated with lower starch in carotenoid-accumulating roots
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MeGBSSI expression was reduced in DXS//PS roots by RNA-seq and qPCR. 2. Reduced expression is consistent with decreased starch accumulation in transgenic roots.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used mainly as a validated marker of starch-biosynthesis suppression rather than as the primary engineering target.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Down-regulated in carotenoid-accumulating storage roots relative to nontransgenic control at 12 MAP
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced expression is associated with lower starch accumulation in carotenoid-engineered roots
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MeSSI expression was reduced in transgenic carotenoid-accumulating roots by RNA-seq and qPCR. 2. Down-regulation is consistent with decreased starch synthesis and dry matter content.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used as a validated endogenous starch-biosynthetic readout in the altered carbon partitioning phenotype.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Up-regulated in carotenoid-accumulating storage roots relative to nontransgenic control at 12 MAP
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with elevated ABA levels in carotenoid-accumulating roots
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NCED3 transcript levels were elevated in transgenic carotenoid-rich roots. 2. Increased expression is consistent with elevated ABA accumulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq differential expression plus ABA quantification
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Endogenous ABA-biosynthetic response gene identified through transcriptome analysis.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Up-regulated in carotenoid-accumulating storage roots relative to nontransgenic control at 12 MAP
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with elevated ABA biosynthesis in carotenoid-accumulating roots
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABA2 transcripts were significantly elevated in engineered carotenoid-rich roots. 2. Up-regulation is consistent with the observed 2–3 fold higher ABA levels.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq differential expression plus ABA quantification
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Downstream ABA-biosynthetic enzyme identified as part of the endogenous response to provitamin A engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses two molecules of geranylgeranyl diphosphate to form phytoene, directing flux from GGDP into carotenoid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: carotenoid biosynthesis via plastid isoprenoid/MEP pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies direct precursor pool for β-carotene; forms upstream committed precursor
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: provitamin A nutrient; likely antioxidant capacity associated with delayed postharvest physiological deterioration
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes strong β-carotene accumulation in cassava storage roots and potato tubers; associated with reduced dry matter and starch and increased soluble sugars, triacylglycerols, fatty acids, and ABA when carotenoids are elevated
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Expression of crtB alone increased cassava root carotenoids to ~25 µg/g DW, above nontransgenic controls. 2. In combination with DXS, carotenoids rose much further, with 40–90 µg/g DW depending on cultivar, mostly as all-trans-β-carotene. 3. Carotenoid accumulation correlated inversely with dry matter and starch content and positively with soluble sugars, oil, and ABA. 4. Similar metabolic shifts were reproduced in potato tubers expressing crtB plus DXS.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic expression in cassava and potato; carotenoid quantification by spectrophotometry and HPLC; metabolite profiling; field and glasshouse phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Glasshouse and field growth; postharvest storage for PPD assessment
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key committed entry step into carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful core enzyme for provitamin A biofortification, especially stacked with DXS; likely should be combined with strategies restoring starch accumulation and limiting β-carotene catabolism
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced dry matter content; reduced starch; altered carbon partitioning toward sugars and lipids
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Bacterial crtB was codon-optimized and fused to a coriander Δ4 acyl-ACP desaturase plastid transit peptide for plastid targeting; in cultivar 60444, reduced DMC also occurred in crtB-only lines, indicating the phenotype tracks carotenoid accumulation rather than DXS specifically.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq of cassava storage roots, GEO: GSE100319

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the first step of the plastid MEP/isoprenoid pathway, forming 1-deoxy-D-xylulose-5-phosphate from pyruvate and glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate, thereby increasing precursor supply for carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: plastid methylerythritol phosphate (MEP) / isoprenoid pathway feeding carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: upstream precursor-supply module for carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies precursor pool for β-carotene biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: provitamin A nutrient; likely antioxidant capacity associated with delayed PPD
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances β-carotene accumulation when co-expressed with crtB, enabling higher provitamin A levels than crtB alone
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Co-expression of DXS with crtB produced the highest carotenoid accumulation among tested engineering strategies. 2. DXS//crtB lines reached 40–90 µg/g DW carotenoids depending on cultivar, with 85–90% as all-trans-β-carotene. 3. Elevated carotenoid accumulation in DXS//crtB lines was associated with lower dry matter and starch and increased sugars, oil, and ABA. 4. Similar effects were observed in potato tubers, indicating a general effect in starch-rich organs.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic co-expression with crtB in cassava and potato; carotenoid and metabolite profiling in glasshouse and field
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Glasshouse and field growth; postharvest storage for PPD assessment
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: First step and key control point of plastid isoprenoid precursor pathway
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Effective precursor-supply gene for stacking with crtB to boost provitamin A; deployment should consider restoring starch/dry matter balance
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: In stacked lines, associated with reduced dry matter and starch and increased sugars/lipids, although DMC reduction was not unique to DXS
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper indicates reduced DMC is associated with carotenoid accumulation itself and is not specific to DXS expression, but DXS substantially boosts carotenoid output when paired with crtB.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq of cassava storage roots, GEO: GSE100319

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Up-regulated in carotenoid-accumulating storage roots of DXS//PS-20 and DXS//PS-37 relative to nontransgenic control at 12 MAP
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates β-carotene to zeaxanthin via xanthophyll intermediates in ABA precursor formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with target-product branch; diverts β-carotene toward ABA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: plant hormone implicated in stress and developmental regulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with increased ABA accumulation in carotenoid-engineered cassava roots and likely drains β-carotene into catabolism
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MeBCH1 expression was significantly elevated in carotenoid-accumulating cassava roots. 2. ABA levels were two- to threefold higher in transgenic roots. 3. The study highlights MeBCH1 as controlling the first step of β-carotene catabolism and as a target for reducing breakdown of provitamin A.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq differential expression linked with metabolite profiling of ABA and β-carotene
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field-grown cassava roots harvested at 12 MAP
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key first step in β-carotene catabolism toward ABA
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate target for down-regulation/editing to reduce β-carotene catabolism and further increase provitamin A retention
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reducing BCH1 may alter ABA homeostasis and stress/development responses
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not itself transgenic; identified through transcriptome analysis as an endogenous target responding to carotenoid enhancement.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Down-regulated in carotenoid-accumulating storage roots of DXS//PS-20 and DXS//PS-37 relative to nontransgenic control at 12 MAP
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts glucose-1-phosphate and ATP to ADP-glucose, the activated glucosyl donor for starch biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor for starch synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: major carbon reserve and determinant of dry matter content
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced expression is associated with lower starch and dry matter content and altered carbon partitioning in carotenoid-accumulating roots
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MeAGPL3 was significantly down-regulated in carotenoid-accumulating cassava roots by RNA-seq and qPCR. 2. Transgenic roots showed 23–37% lower starch and strong dry matter reduction. 3. The study proposes AGPase down-regulation as a likely cause of carbon repartitioning from starch to sugars and oil. 4. Restoring AGPase expression is suggested as a strategy to recover starch while maintaining provitamin A.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR validation; starch/dry matter metabolite phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field-grown cassava roots harvested at 12 MAP
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for carbon partitioning into starch
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value engineering target to restore starch accumulation in provitamin A-biofortified cassava
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increasing AGPase may redirect carbon away from carotenoid and oil accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The Discussion explicitly highlights AGPase genes as potential targets for restoring starch accumulation.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Down-regulated in carotenoid-accumulating storage roots relative to nontransgenic control at 12 MAP
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Together with the AGPase large subunit catalyzes ADP-glucose formation from glucose-1-phosphate and ATP for starch synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor for starch synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: major carbon reserve and determinant of dry matter content
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced expression is associated with reduced starch accumulation and dry matter in provitamin A-engineered cassava roots
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AGPS1 was significantly down-regulated in carotenoid-accumulating roots. 2. The paper identifies AGPase subunit genes as likely contributors to the carbon flux shift away from starch. 3. AGPase is highlighted as a target for restoring starch in engineered cassava.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field-grown cassava roots harvested at 12 MAP
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for starch biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential co-target with AGPL3 for restoring starch in carotenoid-biofortified cassava
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter carbon allocation relative to carotenoid gain
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not qPCR-validated in the paper, but emphasized together with AGPL3 in transcriptomic interpretation.
